Title: Some Ukrainian refugees head home - for dental work
Article:
FOUR years of brutal Russian attacks have forced millions of Ukrainians to seek safety abroad.
But with the war showing no signs of stopping, significant numbers of refugees have started trickling back home, if only temporarily, for one reason: to see their dentist.
Those displaced by an onslaught of Russian tanks, drones and missiles have been navigating long journeys home by car, bus or rail for orthodontic work they prefer – or can only get – inside Ukraine.
"Here in Ukraine, we really appreciate the quality of the medical services, as well as the personalised approach to our daughter," said Yana Sidko, who left for France in 2022.
She now makes the arduous pilgrimage back to the Ukrainian capital with her 13-year-old daughter for treatment.
The country was a dental tourism destination before Russia invaded, with Europeans seeking lower-cost procedures and a broader range of treatment.
But now the vast majority of those coming from abroad are Ukrainians who fled the war, professionals in the industry told AFP.
In a paediatric clinic in Kyiv, dentist Oleg Kovnatskyi told AFP that around 20 per cent of his patients – 10 to 20 people a month – come from abroad.
Around 90 per cent, the 39-year-old said, are Ukrainians.
"Whereas previously there were more foreigners ... today it's our own citizens that left the country following the full-scale invasion, but are now returning," he said.
Some 5.7 million Ukrainian refugees are living outside the country – from a pre-war population of around 40 million.
So common is the trend, there is a running joke that dentists could be one of the main catalysts for many to move back permanently – something that would help alleviate a long-running demographic crisis and a deepening labour shortage.
Refugees return because dentists are cheaper at home and offer some treatment unavailable everywhere – like the use of sedatives for children – Kovnatskyi said.
[[nid:1445002]]
The lack of a language barrier and the fact that children prefer to see the same dentist on repeat visits are also important factors.
"The emotional dimension is essential, as are body language and empathy, and every language has its own nuances," Kovnatskyi said.
"This complexity, made up of linguistic and cultural differences, can be an obstacle that is difficult to overcome," he added.
Patients often come from abroad for procedures that can take several days like X-rays, MRIs and ultrasounds.
Sidko and her 13-year-old daughter, Daryna, travel from France to see Kovnatskyi – their dentist for the past decade.
He has a personal touch and takes Daryna's sensitivity into consideration, she said.
Sidko also schedules her own check-ups on the trips back home.
"It reduces my stress and anxiety, and it's easier to sort things out here, even though the journey is very long and difficult," the 40-year-old told AFP.
She is thinking about returning for good, but only when Russia's daily drone and missile attacks ebb and the situation in Ukraine is better for her daughter.
Kovnatskyi began the visit by replacing one of Daryna's old fillings.
"It won't take very long, we just need to remove the old one and put in a new one," he explained.
Kovnatskyi played upbeat music and reassured Daryna as he got to work.
When the procedure was over, Daryna chose a toy to take home from the clinic.
"People often feel afraid when they don't know what's going on," the teenager told AFP.
"When he explains what's happening, it reduces the fear and you feel in control."
For his part, Kovnatskyi said he would be happy if refugees trickling back home for dental work meant that he and his colleagues had a hand in "bringing Ukraine back to life."
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teen pleads guilty to kicking 10-year-old in the face, fracturing his jaw over fallen bicycle
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A Form Five student pleaded guilty in the magistrate's court here today to causing grievous hurt to a Year Four pupil by kicking him in the face and fracturing his jaw last Friday.
The 17-year-old accused entered the plea after the charge under Section 325 of the Penal Code for voluntarily causing grievous hurt, was read out before magistrate Arun Noval Dass.
According to the charge, he was accused of kicking the 10-year-old boy's jaw and causing injuries at a playground in Tongkang Pechah here at 9pm on May 15.
Section 325 carries a maximum prison sentence of seven years and a fine, upon conviction.
Based on the police report, the victim returned home at 10pm on the day of the incident and complained that he had been assaulted.
The victim's mother then rushed him to the Sultanah Nora Ismail Hospital, where a medical examination revealed that his jaw was fractured.
Police conducted an investigation and detained the teenager to assist in the probe.
Prior to the incident, the victim had leaned his bicycle against a hut near where the accused was sitting.
However, the bicycle fell over and hit the teenager's leg. He then kicked the victim, causing him to fall with a bleeding mouth and a fractured jaw.
Prosecuting officer Inspector Hasniza Che Ibrahim handled the prosecution while the accused was unrepresented.
The court fixed July 1 for the submission of the probation report from the Welfare Department, the presentation of facts, and sentencing.
Pending the mention date, the court allowed the accused bail at RM1,500 with one surety.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Services, F&B SMEs top list of Perkeso non-compliance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs), particularly in the services and food and beverage (F&B) sectors, have been identified as among the employers most negligent in registering and contributing to the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso).
Perkeso Group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Dr Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohammed said the issue of non-compliance has persisted despite the implementation of the Amnesty Month programme and Ops Kesan over the past 17 years.
He said among the common reasons given by employers were a lack of awareness of the obligation to register workers with Perkeso, as well as attempts to reduce operating costs.
"Perkeso has existed for almost 60 years. So the excuse of not knowing that registration and contributions are mandatory is unacceptable.
"Some also claim they want to save costs, even though Perkeso contributions are very low.
"Workers' welfare should never be treated as an area for cost-cutting," he said at a press conference for the launch of Perkeso Amnesty Month.
He said some employers were also confused about the status of contract workers, part-time employees or daily wage workers, resulting in a failure to make contributions.
"However, regardless of the nature of employment, whether daily workers, contract staff or those called in as needed, all must be registered and contributions must be made," he said.
He said statistics from Op Kesan over the past 17 years showed that employer non-compliance remained between 19 and 21 per cent annually.
He said the situation proved that many employers still took workers' social protection lightly.
"If an accident, injury or death occurs, the welfare of workers and their families must be safeguarded. That is why we are serious about enforcement," he said.
He said from May 21 to June 22, Perkeso is giving employers the opportunity to voluntarily come forward to register and contribute for their workers without being subjected to compounds or late contribution interest (FCLB).
However, he said strict action would be taken after the amnesty period ends on June 23 through the implementation of Op Kesan nationwide.
"More than 500 Perkeso officers from 54 offices nationwide will be deployed to carry out Op Kesan.
"There will be no compromise. If employers still fail to register and contribute for their workers, compound notices and prosecution will be initiated," he said.
He added that last year recorded one of the highest numbers of enforcement actions involving non-compliant employers.
He said a total of 5,099 cases were issued compounds amounting to RM3.58 million, while 4,394 cases were prosecuted in court in 2025.
"Last year was among the highest in terms of prosecuted and compounded cases compared with previous years. Most involved small companies, SMEs, the services industry and restaurants," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hospitalised Thai princess's condition 'deteriorated'
Article:
BANGKOK: The health condition of a Thai princess who has been hospitalised for more than three years has deteriorated, the palace said Thursday.
Princess Bajrakitiyabha Mahidol, the king's eldest daughter, fell ill during a military dog training session in December 2022 and has remained in hospital since.
She suffered from a severe blood infection and her lung and kidney functions were being supported by medical devices and medication, the palace previously said.
The Bureau of the Royal Household said in a statement on Thursday that the princess had developed an abdominal infection from inflammation of the large intestine and was experiencing "unstable vital signs, low blood pressure, irregular heartbeat and abnormal blood coagulation."
The palace added that Bajrakitiyabha was still relying on medical equipment to support her lung and kidney functions, as well as medication.
"Her condition has continued to deteriorate," it said, adding that the infection had become "uncontrollable and affected other organs."
Educated in Britain, the United States and Thailand, Bajrakitiyabha held several positions with the United Nations and campaigned for better treatment of women in prisons.
Known in Thailand as "Princess Bha", the 47-year-old is the only child from King Maha Vajiralongkorn's first marriage.
She is seen as close to her father and was appointed to a senior role in his bodyguard command a year before her hospitalisation.
The 73-year-old king, who has seven children from four marriages, has not announced his chosen heir, though succession rules favour men.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Illegal Jeli gold panning site shut before fatal landslide
Article:
KOTA BARU: The authorities had sealed off a stream near the Jerimbong oil palm plantation in Jeli yesterday, before two men were later buried in a landslide while allegedly carrying out illegal gold mining activities.
The stream, located near Kampung Jerimbong in Kuala Balah, is believed to be an illegal mining site that has not been gazetted by the Kelantan government for such activities.
A source said the area was believed to contain large gold deposits and had attracted many locals over the past few months.
"It is understood that several personnel from the Jeli District Council sealed off the area at about 3pm yesterday as the location was considered high-risk and dangerous.
"However, shortly after the operation, several locals, including the three men involved in the incident, are believed to have returned to the stream to continue their gold mining activities," the source said.
He said the three friends had been carrying out the activity for more than two hours before the landslide occurred, killing two of them.
Another man reportedly managed to escape before seeking help from the authorities.
It is understood that this was the first fatal incident reported since the state government decided to allow the public to pan for gold in designated areas last year.
Meanwhile, Kelantan deputy Menteri Besar Datuk Mohamed Fadzli Hassan said the relevant state authorities would investigate the matter.
Last November, Fadzli said residents in Kelantan would be allowed to pan for gold on
state-owned land
, with licences issued under strict conditions.
He added that the initiative would allow residents to directly benefit from Kelantan's natural resources.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Psychiatrist: Parents, peers given greater weight in Zara's psychological autopsy report
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Testimonies from the late Zara Qairina Mahathir's extended family members carried less weight in the psychological autopsy report compared with those of her parents and peers, the Coroner's Court heard today.
Consultant forensic psychiatrist Dr Chua Sze Hung testified that while interviewing multiple informants was crucial, the selection of interviewees needed to be tailored individually for adolescents.
"The informants are normally from the peer group, as, in this case, the incident happened in a hostel.
"The bulk of informants came from there. The priority beyond the live-in parents should be the peers," the recalled 67th witness told coroner Amir Shah Amir Hassan on the 71st day of the inquest.
He said the other family members did not live with the deceased and only had occasional contact and interaction with her.
"When I mentioned live-in, it must be highlighted that Zara's peers in SMKA Tun Datu Mustapha literally stayed in the hostel with her. Some of them shared the same dormitory," he said.
Lawyer Rizwandean M. Borhan had questioned the expert on the reasons for excluding Zara Qairina's uncle Abu Bakar Lamat, aunt Nur Shira Abdullah, another relative, and her primary school teachers from the interviews.
Dr Chua said he decided that interviews with Zara Qairina's mother and father were sufficient to examine her upbringing and her relationship with her parents.
Regarding the primary school teachers, Dr Chua said he chose instead to interview several of Zara Qairina's classmates as they spent more time with her in class.
He added that he had interviewed 40 people for the report, having identified them in two stages using the investigation paper summary.
"First, around 20 informants were identified from the summary. As the interviews progressed, relevant names mentioned by other informants assisted us to further
identify people
related to the incident," he said.
Zara Qairina, 13, was found unconscious on the ground floor of SMKA Tun Datu Mustapha on July 16, and pronounced dead the next day.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zeti calls for more investment in education and skills
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Former Bank Negara Malaysia governor Tan Sri Dr Zeti Akhtar Aziz has called for greater investment in talent development and education, cautioning that Malaysia's future competitiveness will depend on how well it prepares its workforce for a rapidly changing global economy.
Speaking at the FLUX Business Forum 2026, Zeti said talent management is one of the most important factors in building long-term economic resilience, especially as businesses face growing uncertainty from geopolitical tensions, supply chain disruptions and technological change.
"The investment in education is a vital infrastructure investment that will determine our future growth potential," she said.
She added that a more skilled workforce would help Malaysia improve productivity, create new opportunities and strengthen the country's competitiveness.
The former governor also stressed the need for Malaysia's education system to adapt to the changing demands of the economy, as workers today require different skills compared with previous decades.
"This is why education, in my view, is the most important ministry that will drive the future potential of our economy," Zeti added.
She added on the importance of economic flexibility for Malaysia, citing that it depends on having the right talent to help businesses and institutions adjust to changing conditions.
"It's about, essentially, reinvention," she said.
Businesses, policymakers and financial institutions must remain agile and continue adapting to new realities instead of relying on outdated systems and ways of thinking, Zeti added.
She cautioned that global competition for skilled workers is becoming more intense and Malaysia's education institutions must produce talent suited for the new economic environment.
"Having the agility to adjust, having the flexibility to respond and the adaptability to reinvent according to the different needs is part of the new normal," Zeti said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: A-G opposes Malaysian Bar's challenge against Zahid's NFA status
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Attorney-General (A-G) is opposing the Malaysian Bar's bid to challenge the decision to classify Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi's Yayasan Akalbudi case as No Further Action (NFA).
Senior federal counsel Nurhafizza Azizan, appearing for the A-G, told the High Court during case management of the Bar's leave application to commence judicial review proceedings before Judge Alice Loke Yee Ching today.
After hearing submissions from both parties, Loke fixed June 30 to decide whether the proceedings should continue or be stayed pending the disposal of related appeals at the Federal Court.
According to court documents, the Bar claimed that the A-G's decision to grant an NFA to the Umno president was irrational, unreasonable, and contrary to the public interest.
It also argued that the decision lacked adequate reasons or sufficient supporting material, and was contrary to established prosecutorial guidelines.
The Bar is seeking a court order to compel the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC) to recharge Zahid over all 47 charges of criminal breach of trust, corruption and money laundering involving Yayasan Akalbudi funds.
The Bar filed the application naming the A-G and Zahid as the first and second respondents, respectively.
On Jan 8, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission said that after reviewing all materials, new evidence and the results of further investigations, including prosecutorial assessments, it found that the available evidence was insufficient to support the continuation of the prosecution on all charges.
On Jan 12, the A-G said the decision to classify Zahid's corruption case as NFA was made only after carefully reviewing the six representations submitted by the Bagan Datuk member of parliament.
He said the Attorney-General's Chambers did not act arbitrarily, noting that the representations raised issues of fact and law which required further examination.
On Feb 3, Zahid applied for a full acquittal following the A-GC's media statement on Jan 8 declaring that no further action would be taken on the charges.
On Sept 4, 2023, then High Court judge Datuk Collin Lawrence Sequerah, now a Federal Court judge, granted a discharge not amounting to an acquittal to the 73-year-old politician in relation to the case.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Late teen's diaries provide more material than family recordings, psychiatrist tells Zara Qairina inquest
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The diaries of the late 13-year-old Zara Qairina Mahathir provide more material evidence of her internal mental state than audio recordings or verbal interactions with family members, the Coroner's Court heard today.
Consultant forensic psychiatrist Dr Chua Sze Hung testified that verbal interactions alone are a less accurate indicator of an individual's psychological well-being.
"It is common for an adolescent to mask their distress. They would present an outwardly pleasant presence, and there is a risk of presenting a false self.
"In contrast, the diary is the most genuine self-representation, free of social and interpersonal pressure and judgment. It is a raw, private, and unfiltered self. The diary would be more material," the 67th witness testified before coroner Amir Shah Amir Hassan.
Dr Chua was replying to coordinating officer Dacia Jane Romanus regarding the materiality of the evidence on the 71st day of the inquest into Zara's death.
The recalled witness was provided with three witness statements, from Zara's uncle, Abu Bakar Lamat,
aunt Nur Shira Abdullah
, and another relative regarding their observations of the relationship between Zara and her mother, Noraidah Lamat.
All three relatives testified in court this week.
Dacia also requested a recorded phone conversation between the mother and daughter to be played for Dr Chua.
The witness reiterated that a diary was a safe space for Zara to process her true emotions.
"I would like to rehighlight that conflicts can be intermittent or pervasive. It can be a single event, repetitive, or chronic.
"The psychological pain is real, given that it had reached a point where she wished for her own death and the death of her parent," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 2025 records highest Perkeso prosecutions and compounds in five years
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Social Security Organisation (Perkeso) recorded its highest number of prosecutions and compounds in five years in 2025, with penalties amounting to RM3.58 million.
Perkeso group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Dr Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohammed said 5,099 compound cases and 4,536 prosecution cases had been recorded this year, surpassing the 2024 figures of 4,394 compounds and 3,677 prosecutions.
He said data as of April showed there were 915,616 active registered employers nationwide, but only 74 per cent were actively contributing for their workers, leaving 238,060 employers, or 26 per cent, non-compliant.
"This shows there are still employers who take workers' welfare lightly despite repeated enforcement and awareness efforts over the years," he said at a press conference today.
He added that between 2020 and 2025, Perkeso issued 19,190 compound notices worth RM11.9 million and prosecuted 17,686 cases under the Employees' Social Security Act 1969 and the Employment Insurance System Act 2017.
At the event, Mohammed Azman also launched Perkeso's 17th Grace Period programme, giving employers until June 22 to voluntarily register their businesses and workers without facing compounds, prosecution, or late payment contribution charges.
He said the month-long grace period was the final opportunity for employers to comply before nationwide enforcement operations begin.
Mohammed Azman said Perkeso remained concerned about foreign worker protection, revealing that 495,380 foreign workers had registration records but no contribution records between 2019 and April this year.
He said this year's enforcement would focus particularly on employers hiring foreign workers, especially in cases involving outsourced labour through employment agencies.
"Whether workers are employed directly or through agencies, both parties are responsible for ensuring they are registered and contributions are paid," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MCMC issues statutory demand to TikTok over offensive content
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) has issued a statutory demand to TikTok over the platform's failure to act promptly and adequately against offensive and defamatory content, as well as fake accounts targeting the royal institution.
In a statement today, MCMC said the action was prompted by the circulation of content associated with an account purporting to be linked to His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia.
"The content in question includes material that is grossly offensive, false, menacing and insulting in nature, including AI-generated videos and manipulated images, which may contravene Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, as well as other  laws in Malaysia.
"Despite prior notifications and engagements, TikTok's moderation response has been found to be unsatisfactory, particularly in ensuring the prompt removal of such content and preventing the further dissemination of harmful material," the statement said.
MCMC said the statutory demand requires TikTok to undertake immediate remedial measures, including strengthening its content moderation mechanisms, ensuring more effective enforcement against content that violates Malaysian laws and community standards, and providing a formal explanation regarding its moderation failures.
The commission said it views seriously any misuse of online platforms to disseminate content that is grossly offensive, false, menacing or detrimental to public order, particularly when it involves the institution of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong and the Malay Rulers.
"Such matters fall within the broader context of 3R (race, religion and royalty) issues, which are highly sensitive and may undermine public order, national harmony and respect for constitutional institutions."
The commission said social media service providers operating in Malaysia are expected to uphold a higher level of responsibility in preventing their platforms from being misused for unlawful and harmful activities.
"Social media platforms must act responsibly and swiftly in addressing content that is unlawful, harmful, or poses a threat to public order, especially on matters involving 3R (race, religion and royalty) sensitivities.
"MCMC will continue to take firm and proportionate action when necessary to ensure digital platforms operating in Malaysia fulfil their responsibilities in maintaining a safe, secure and respectful online environment."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mother of 3 among trio pleads guilty to robbery, stripping of woman
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A mother of three was among the accused who pleaded guilty today to robbery, wrongful confinement and assault involving a woman forced to strip and filmed.
Nur Syuhaidah Saharan, 34; Aliza Khamis, 45; and Mohamad Salehuddin Ali, 34, were named as those involved.
Salehuddin was charged with four offences, Nur Syuhaidah with three, and Aliza with one.
They pleaded guilty before Judge Osman Affendi Mohd Shalleh and magistrate Arun Noval Dass.
In the Sessions Court, Salehuddin and Nur Syuhaidah were charged, together with 2 accomplices still at large, with gang robbery using a knife involving RM4,200 and a mobile phone belonging to a 33-year-old woman.
The offence is punishable under Section 395 of the Penal Code for gang robbery, read with Section 397, which carries up to 20 years' imprisonment and whipping upon conviction.
In the same court, Nur Syuhaidah was also charged with testing positive for amphetamine and methamphetamine for the third time.
She was charged under Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, punishable under Section 39C(1)(b) for repeat drug use, read with Section 38B for enhanced punishment.
Nur Syuhaidah, a mother of 3 pleaded guilty to the drug charge and was sentenced to 5 years' imprisonment, followed by 2 years' supervision after release.
In the magistrate's court, Aliza, Nur Syuhaidah and Salehuddin were jointly charged with wrongful confinement of the same victim, together with accomplices.
They were charged under Section 340 of the Penal Code for wrongful confinement, punishable under Section 342, read with Section 34 for common intention.
Nur Syuhaidah and Salehuddin were also charged under Section 509 of the Penal Code for insulting the modesty of the victim by recording videos and taking photographs of her naked.
Salehuddin was separately charged with repeatedly punching the victim on the face, body, legs, chest and stomach, causing injuries.
For this offence, the father of two was charged under Section 323 of the Penal Code, read with Section 34, which carries up to one year's imprisonment, a fine of up to RM2,000, or both.
Deputy public prosecutor Sharifah Natasha Syed Ahmad prosecuted both cases, while the accused were represented by National Legal Aid Foundation counsel Tan Song Yan.
Following the guilty pleas, the court fixed tomorrow for sentencing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perkeso gives grace period until June 22 for non-compliant employers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Social Security Organisation (Perkeso) has launched a special amnesty programme giving employers until June 22 to voluntarily register and contribute for workers without facing legal action.
During the grace period, employers who come forward will not face compounds, prosecution, or Late Contribution Payment Fines (FCLB), in a move aimed at improving compliance under the Employees' Social Security Act 1969 (Act 4).
Perkeso group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Dr Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohammed said the initiative was introduced after enforcement data showed employer non-compliance remained high at about 26 per cent.
"As of April, there were 915,616 active registered employers, but only 74 per cent, or 677,556 employers, were contributing for their workers.
"This means about 238,060 employers are still non-compliant," he said during the launch of the Grace Period programme at Perkeso Tower, Jalan Ampang, today.
Mohammed Azman said findings from Op Kesan also showed that between 19 and 21 per cent of workers checked over the years were still not registered with Perkeso.
"In other words, one out of every five workers screened under Op Kesan was found to be without Perkeso contributions," he said.
He added that from June 23, Perkeso would deploy more than 500 personnel from its 54 offices nationwide under Ops Kesan to identify and take action against employers who fail to register and contribute for their workers.
Mohammed Azman said 495,380 foreign workers registered with Perkeso since 2019 had no contribution records.
He said Selangor recorded the highest number of such cases at 37 per cent, followed by Kuala Lumpur (22 per cent), Johor (10 per cent), Sarawak (six per cent), and Penang (four per cent).
He added that the construction industry recorded the highest non-compliance rate at 21.4 per cent, followed by manufacturing (20.9 per cent), other services (12.6 per cent), agriculture, forestry and fisheries (9.1 per cent), and retail (5.3 per cent).
Mohammed Azman said Perkeso would expand enforcement efforts this year to cover domestic and gig workers, both of whom are required to be covered under Perkeso.
A total of 892,000 gig workers have registered with Perkeso since the implementation of the gig workers framework on March 31, with the majority from the e-hailing and p-hailing sectors.
"However, our approach remains a soft landing, taking into account current economic uncertainties and the global environment," he said.
Failure to register employees could result in compounds of up to RM5,000. Employers could also face a six per cent annual late payment charge, court action, fines of up to RM10,000, imprisonment of up to two years, or both.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foreign scam syndicates exploiting tourist passes to enter Malaysia, says police
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Foreign-run scam syndicates are increasingly exploiting tourist and social visit passes to enter Malaysia and operate online fraud networks to evade detection.
Selangor police chief Datuk Shazeli Kahar said investigations revealed that several foreign suspects arrested in recent raids in Sepang had entered the country as early as the beginning of the year, March, and April.
He said police are conducting cross-checks with the Immigration Department to verify the travel documents and immigration status of all suspects involved.
"So far, investigations have found that some entered using tourist passes while others used social visit passes.
"For many, this was their first time entering Malaysia before they were detained," he told a press conference at the Selangor police headquarters today.
Shazeli said three separate operations carried out by the Sepang district commercial crime investigation department successfully crippled several foreign-linked syndicates involved in online investment scams and financial data collection activities.
In the first raid on May 19 at 11pm, police stormed a residential unit in Bandar 16 Sierra, Puchong, and arrested 11 foreigners, comprising 10 men and one woman aged between 18 and 63.
Police seized eight laptops, eight mobile phones and a router, with the total value of the items estimated at RM36,000.
Initial investigations found the syndicate had been operating since March, with the woman acting as a 'cook' while the 10 men served as operators.
"They offered online investment schemes targeting Peruvian victims through Telegram and specific websites," he said.
In the second operation conducted the same night at 11.45pm at another residential premises in Puchong, police detained seven foreign men aged between 27 and 36, believed to be involved in another online investment scam syndicate.
Among the items seized were a monitor, two CPUs, a laptop, two mobile phones and a WiFi router, with the total seizure estimated at RM16,000.
Shazeli said the syndicate had been active since April, with all suspects acting as operators offering investment schemes to victims in China through several online applications.
The third raid was carried out on May 20 at 1.30am at a house in Puchong, where police arrested 11 foreigners, comprising 10 men and one woman aged between 31 and 36.
Police seized three monitors, three CPUs, three mobile phones and a WiFi router worth about RM17,000.
"The syndicate, which has been operating since April, is believed to be involved in collecting financial information obtained through fraudulent activities.
"All suspects acted as account managers who received financial data linked to scam activities targeting Chinese nationals before uploading the information into the syndicate's system through specific websites," he said.
Shazeli said the personal data collected would then be shared with other syndicate groups for manipulation and further scam activities.
All three cases are being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating and Section 120B of the Penal Code for criminal conspiracy.
"All suspects have been remanded until May 23 to assist investigations.
"We urge the public to channel information to police if they suspect residential premises or business centres are being used as scam operation hubs," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan-Malaysia partnership matters more than ever
Article:
MALAYSIAN Defence Minister  Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin announced on May 19 that Putrajaya is seeking compensation of more than RM1 billion after Norway withdrew export approvals for a naval ‌strike missile system.
The unexpected cancellation, which was due to enhance the capabilities of Malaysian combat ships, was a stark reminder of the need for steady partners in an ever more unsteady world.
Japan stands out as a nation willing, and able, to play that role, having enacted its most significant arms export reforms in decades, from which Putrajaya stands to benefit directly.
Japan has already begun collaborating with Malaysia in non-military spheres too, addressing China's pressure campaign on rare earth supply chains.
In February, Tokyo embarked on its first technical cooperation project with Putrajaya focused on critical mineral development, reflecting a vital understanding that economic resilience and strategic autonomy are deeply intertwined.
Unfortunately, Beijing's coercion is not restricted to economic measures, and China now persistently violates the sovereignty of Indo-Pacific states.
Last year, the Chinese Coast Guard (CCG) spent 261 calendar days in the northeastern area of Malaysia's Exclusive Economic Zone.
It has maintained a regular maritime presence near the Luconia Shoals as it looks to increase pressure on Malaysia's sovereign oil exploration activities in the region.
The CCG has also been assertive in Philippine waters near the Scarborough Shoal and around Japan's Senkaku islands, reaching a record high in 2025.
These trends are supplemented by Beijing's large-scale land reclamation programme, as well as its militarisation of features across the South China Sea, including most recently at Antelope Reef.
Historically, Malaysia and Japan have benefited from a US security presence that helped balance against China, even as Putrajaya has remained cautious about formal alignment with Washington and enthusiastic for close economic cooperation with Beijing.
And while the US remains committed and militarily present in the region, the conflict in Iran means its immediate focus currently lies elsewhere.
The combination of Washington's reorientation and the scale and complexity of today's Indo-Pacific challenges make this an urgent moment for regional nations to step up and contribute to upholding stability and the rules-based order.
This creates both the opportunity and the responsibility for capable partners, such as Japan and Malaysia, to shoulder a greater share of the burden for Indo-Pacific security.
The Japan-Malaysia partnership is underpinned by a shared understanding of what the regional order should look like.
The Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP) vision upheld by Tokyo, and Asean's Outlook on the Indo-Pacific actively championed by Putrajaya, both emphasise principles such as freedom of navigation and opposition to coercion.
Yet Putrajaya's approach to promoting this vision is shaped by its circumstances.
Deep economic interdependence with China and public scepticism of the Trump administration limit how far and how fast Malaysia can harden its military posture over obvious infractions of its sovereignty.
Japan, by contrast, is responding to the changing regional order with great urgency.
Backed by overwhelming public support following a landslide election in February, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's administration has increased defence spending to a record ¥9 trillion (RM223.12 billion) and authorised the purchase of weapons including tomahawk missiles for its destroyers.
Just last month, Tokyo also removed restrictions on arms exports, enabling the transfer of offensive weaponry to 17 partner countries, including Malaysia.
Australia has already taken advantage of Japan's defence export capabilities by ordering 11 best-in-class Mogami-class frigates.
As threats to its maritime sovereignty continue to grow, Malaysia may also consider pursuing a similar arrangement on a smaller scale.
Tokyo has already provided Malaysia with surveillance and monitoring equipment in the past, and further utilisation of such cooperation frameworks would also be highly beneficial.
The Japan–Malaysia Comprehensive Strategic Partnership, signed in December 2023, facilitated Putrajaya's inclusion on the exclusive 17-nation list. It has also provided the institutional backbone for Japan and Malaysia's growing cooperation.
Dialogues between the two nations' leaders, and bilateral naval exercises such as MALPAN, which involved air defence exercises that improved interoperability in case of maritime conflict, show that Putrajaya and Tokyo are steadily building habits of cooperation and operational trust.
Japan–Malaysia cooperation does not operate in isolation, nor could it alone deter Chinese threats to the international order or absorb the shockwaves sent across the region by the Iran war.
Rather, it complements a broader web of regional security relationships, including the Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP) vision, the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP), and Asean-centred mechanisms, all of which help anchor regional diplomacy.
The MALPAN exercise, cooperation on rare earth supply chains, and institutional frameworks now in place are all evidence of deepening cooperation between Japan and Malaysia.
If both countries are to preserve a regional status quo that safeguards their economic interests and strategic autonomy, their collaboration must deepen.
* The writer is principal consultant at Arunachala Research & Consultancy Sdn Bhd (ARRESCON), a think tank specialising in strategic and geopolitical analysis
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Speculation mounts over possible early Johor state election
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Speculation is mounting that Johor could be heading towards an early state election following talk of a special state assembly sitting on June 24, with political circles claiming invitations have already been circulated.
Screenshots of WhatsApp messages have also gone viral, claiming the
special sitting
will take place on June 24.
The New Straits Times contacted the Johor Menteri Besar's office, but was told it could not confirm the matter at this point.
The rumours have intensified whispers that the special sitting could be linked to a possible dissolution of the state assembly to pave the way for fresh state polls.
While no official confirmation has been issued, sources within Johor Umno said discussions surrounding a potential dissolution had become increasingly active over the past several days.
Several party insiders remained guarded, with some declining to comment directly on whether the state assembly would be dissolved soon.
"There are talks, but at this stage, nothing has been officially announced. Everyone is waiting for the Menteri Besar's next move," said a source familiar with the discussions.
Another source claimed the speculation was driven partly by growing confidence within sections of Johor Barisan Nasional (BN), which is believed to be satisfied with its current political positioning and grassroots strength.
The rumours come amid wider national political manoeuvring as parties continue recalibrating strategies ahead of future electoral contests.
The NST also contacted Johor State Assembly Speaker Datuk Mohd Puad Zarkashi, who did not respond at press time.
Meanwhile, Pasir Gudang member of parliament Hassan Abdul Karim said he had not received an invitation to attend the purported special sitting.
"There is a strong possibility that if a special state assembly sitting is held on June 24, the agenda or proceedings may involve the dissolution of the Johor State Assembly," he said.
He added that the state assembly's five-year term would expire in 2027, making an early dissolution a strong possibility if the special sitting proceeds.
Hassan also pointed out that any dissolution of the assembly would first need to be presented to the Sultan of Johor for consent, either on or before June 24.
"The Menteri Besar is responsible for advising the Sultan of Johor and obtaining the ruler's consent for the dissolution. It is unclear whether this process has already taken place," he said.
He added that if the Sultan consented to the dissolution, it would be appropriate for the assembly to table a motion regarding the matter on June 24.
Hassan also said the assembly proceedings primarily concerned the 56 Johor assemblymen, as well as relevant parties, including the media.
Johor held its last state election in March 2022, which saw BN secure a commanding victory after winning 40 out of 56 seats.
Checks at Bangunan Sultan Ismail, where the state assembly is usually held, did not show any visible signs of preparation for a special sitting, while assembly officials said they were unaware of any such arrangements.
For now, Johor political leaders remain publicly cautious even as speculation continues to swirl over whether the southern state will become the focus of a fresh electoral contest.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Turkey passes law to halve corporate tax for manufacturing companies
Article:
ANKARA: Turkey's parliament passed a law that cuts corporate tax for manufacturing companies, introduces incentives to repatriate assets held abroad and extends tax advantages for financial services exports, according to the legislation.
Under the law, corporate tax for manufacturing companies will be reduced to 12.5 per cent, from the current 25 per cent.
An initial proposal of the law had envisaged 9.0 per cent for manufacturing exporters and 14 per cent for other exporters.
The law includes a scheme allowing money, gold, foreign exchange and securities held abroad to be brought into Turkey until July 31, 2027.
Assets brought in under the scheme will be taxed 5.0 per cent, but no tax will apply if they are held for five years in certain financial instruments.
The law extends a 100 per cent corporate tax exemption on financial services export income at the Istanbul Financial Centre, a hub for banks and financial companies in central Istanbul, until 2047.
The law grants a 20-year income tax exemption on foreign-sourced income for individuals choosing to relocate to Turkey.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ministry steps up financing aid amid Middle East tensions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry has rolled out a range of financing support measures to help industries cope with the ongoing global supply crisis and geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.
Its deputy minister Sim Tze Tzin said the assistance, particularly targeted at SMEs, is aimed at easing cash flow pressures and supporting the shift towards alternative supply chains and energy sources.
It is also intended to shield exporters from growing international market volatility while maintaining the competitiveness and resilience of local industries.
"As far as we understand, industry players have informed us that logistics costs have risen by as much as 50 per cent. On average, the cost has gone up to about five to 15 per cent on average.
"However, the government understands these challenges. That is why the ministry has consolidated all available initiatives and announced them together, so SMEs know that we are not leaving them behind," he said at a press conference today.
One of the initiatives announced is the Sustainable and Green Biz Financing facility by Malaysian Industrial Development Finance Bhd offering financing of up to RM10 million.
Under the scheme, SMEs can obtain financing at a two per cent interest rate, while non-SMEs are eligible for financing at five per cent interest.
The facility is aimed at accelerating the adoption of green manufacturing practices and Battery Energy Storage Systems.
To strengthen export resilience, Sim said Export-Import Bank of Malaysia has rolled out several targeted support measures in response to the Middle East conflict.
These include the Malaysia Global Connect Go Export Cover, which provides a 30 per cent incentive on takaful contribution costs capped at RM25,000 to protect exporters against commercial and political risks.
The bank is also offering the Skim Eksport Lonjakan, a RM500 million fund that offers one per cent annual profit rate subsidy for up to seven years to help local companies expand their international capabilities.
In addition, exporters can access trade credit takaful and insurance protection against risks such as non-payment, insolvency and political disruptions.
On emergency relief measures, Sim said Bank Negara Malaysia has allocated RM5 billion under the SME Stabilisation Relief Facility, which became available on May 15 this year.
He said existing MIDF clients affected by the Middle East crisis can apply for moratoriums or financing restructuring of up to 12 months, including the conversion of revolving facilities into term financing to enhance long-term affordability.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Boys sexually abused at Gombak welfare home now 'a shell of their former selves', says family
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The two boys who were sexually abused at an unlicensed children's welfare home in Gombak have yet to recover from the trauma and are now a shell of their former selves.
The victims' family members said the children are finding it hard to come to terms with what happened to them.
The mother of one of the boys said her son had become withdrawn and fearful following the abuse.
"He is traumatised and no longer behaves like he used to," she said.
An aunt to one of the victims told the court that the children continued to suffer emotionally and are struggling to cope with the ordeal.
The women were delivering their victim impact statement before the Sessions Court handed down an 11-year jail sentence and five strokes of the rotan on the welfare home's caretaker today.
Judge Norshila Kamaruddin meted out the sentence to 25-year-old
Retna Velu
, who pleaded guilty to six sexual offences involving children.
Retna, clad in a blue T-shirt, remained silent throughout the proceedings. He broke down in tears when the judge asked him to appeal for a lenient sentence.
Several of the victims' family members were seen in tears during the proceedings, while others appeared visibly upset after the sentence was delivered.
Lawyer Kirthen Subramaniam, who appeared as a watching brief for all the victims' families and Pertubuhan Kebangkitan Tiga Tangan Selangor, said his clients felt the jail sentence was insufficient given the severity of the case and the lasting impact on the victims.
He said the victims, who are still traumatised, are unable to move on from the ordeal and continue to struggle with the emotional and psychological effects of the abuse.
"The victims are still young... it is hard for them to come to terms with what happened and to focus on their studies.
"They are now afraid to talk and have proper communication. They are also now fearful of speaking to strangers," he said when met after the proceeding at the Ampang Sessions Court.
On April 9, Retna pleaded guilty to charges involving physical and non-physical sexual abuse under the Sexual Offences Against Children Act, as well as committing carnal intercourse against the order of nature under Section 377C of the Penal Code.
The offences were committed at the welfare home in Batu Caves between November last year and March this year, involving boys aged between eight and 13.
The court ordered the sentences to run concurrently from the date of his arrest.
New Straits Times previously reported that at least 12 out of 36 occupants of the home had been
subjected to abuse
.
Medical examination conducted also showed old and new injuries aligned with the alleged sexual abuse of some male children.
Police arrested and remanded four suspects, including the home's 31-year-old owner and three other workers aged between 17 and 25.
Initial checks found the home was not registered with the Social Welfare Department and had instead been operating under a private company.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt aims to table Senior Citizens Bill in next Parliament session
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government targets to table the Senior Citizens Bill in the next Parliament session, subject to procedural coordination and further review by the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC).
Women, Family and Community Development Minister Datuk Seri Nancy Shukri said the draft bill has been prepared, but stakeholders are still being consulted to ensure diverse views are taken into consideration before it is finalised.
"The bill is expected to include provisions on parental care responsibilities and the welfare of senior citizens to address the country's growing ageing population.
"We are hoping for the next Parliament session because the draft is ready. It is now a matter of procedures with other ministries and resubmitting it to the A-GC for guidance and further input on areas that require improvement," she said.
She added that the consultation process is ongoing as more parties want their views included, which she noted is a positive sign that the public cares about the welfare of senior citizens.
Nancy said this to reporters after officiating at the Malaysian Care Ecosystem Seminar and Stakeholder Consultation here today.
The event, titled "Assuring the Quality of Caregivers", was organised by the ministry, the National Council of Women's Organisations Malaysia, and the Universiti Malaya Faculty of Medicine at the Kuala Lumpur Convention Centre.
The minister said discussions on the bill are also being conducted on a bipartisan basis through a parliamentary select committee to garner cross-party support.
On concerns over rising elderly caregiving costs, she said the government currently provides tax relief for children caring for elderly parents at home, including support for people with disabilities.
She added that the government is studying proposals to assist families who place elderly parents in private care homes, with recommendations submitted to the Finance Ministry for consideration.
The ministry also plans to provide professional training for its Welfare Department officers, affiliates, and external providers to improve caregiving standards.
In her speech earlier, Nancy said Malaysia is undergoing a major demographic transition and is expected to become an ageing nation by 2040, when those aged 65 and above will exceed 15 per cent of the total population.
She said that as of 2025, the elderly population had surpassed 2.9 million.
Falling fertility rates, smaller households, and rising female labour force participation continue to drive the demand for long-term care and community-based caregiving services.
"This year, the ministry targets to train 2,000 caregivers, including Welfare Department officers, caregivers for bedridden individuals, and community-based providers," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sarawak ramps up business events with infrastructure push, aviation connectivity
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sarawak is strengthening its position as a regional business events hub through strategic investments in infrastructure, aviation connectivity and industry partnerships, as it shifts from destination marketing towards long-term economic impact and sector development.
The state is banking on large-scale infrastructure upgrades and ecosystem development to capture greater opportunities in the global meetings, incentives, conventions and exhibitions industry, particularly as business travel and international conferences continue to recover post-pandemic.
A key catalyst is the upcoming expansion of the Borneo Convention Centre Kuching (BCCK), which is expected to be completed in 2028, and the introduction of AirBorneo, Sarawak's regional airline.
With BCCK2, improved air connectivity and stronger institutional collaboration in the pipeline, Sarawak appears to be positioning business events not only as a tourism driver, but as a platform to attract investment, support industrial growth and strengthen its broader economic transformation agenda.
Sarawak's growing ambitions were highlighted through its participation at the IMEX Frankfurt 2026 for the fourth consecutive year, reflecting a broader strategy to position the state as Malaysia and Borneo's "legacy capital" for business events.
The state's presence at the global trade exhibition aligns with the state's post-pandemic development agenda under the Post Covid-19 Development Strategy 2030 (PCDS 2030), which prioritises sustainability, innovation and long-term economic value creation.
Hii Chang Kee, chairman of Business Events Sarawak (BESarawak), said the state's international engagement is increasingly focused on attracting collaborations that generate broader economic benefits rather than merely promoting Sarawak as an event venue.
He said Sarawak is positioning itself as a strategic partner for event organisers by supporting sustainability and legacy initiatives, while facilitating opportunities linked to investment, innovation, talent development and sectoral growth.
Beyond physical infrastructure, the state is also investing in "soft infrastructure" to deepen industry linkages and knowledge transfer. Among these efforts is an academic mobility programme that connects universities, industry stakeholders and international partners through technical visits, study exchanges and collaborative learning initiatives aligned with Sarawak's economic priority sectors.
Acting BESarawak chief executive officer Jason Tan Chin Foo said such initiatives are aimed at reinforcing Sarawak's long-term competitiveness in business events while creating wider benefits for communities and economic sectors.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang says food supply sufficient ahead of Aidiladha, launches local vegetable campaign
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Supplies of essential food items in Penang remain sufficient ahead of Hari Raya Aidiladha, with prices of controlled goods still below the ceiling price.
Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the state government continued to monitor the supply and pricing of essential goods through regular executive council meetings to ensure consumers were not burdened by excessive price hikes.
"Supplies of the 27 controlled items remain sufficient and prices are still below the ceiling level," he said after launching a campaign promoting locally grown vegetables at Komtar.
The campaign, organised by the state government through the Agrotechnology, Food Security and Cooperative Development Committee together with the Federal Agricultural Marketing Authority (FAMA) and the Agriculture Department, aims to encourage consumers to buy local produce while helping farmers secure better returns.
Meanwhile, state Agrotechnology, Food Security and Cooperative Development Committee chairman Datuk Rashidi Zinol said local vegetable growers had been struggling with low farmgate prices despite higher retail prices.
"We want Penangites to support and consume local vegetables. Farmers have faced difficulties because farmgate prices had fallen too low," he said.
Rashidi said vegetables supplied under the campaign were sourced from farms in Penang, including those under the Permanent Food Production Park (TKPM) programme, where pesticide and fertiliser use is monitored by the Agriculture Department.
He said the initiative would ensure consumers had access to safer and fresher produce while helping farmers cope with rising production costs.
According to Rashidi, farmgate prices for vegetables had improved in recent weeks, rising from between RM1.20 and RM1.90 per kilogramme to around RM2.50 and RM3.
However, he noted that some vegetables continued to retail at prices as high as RM4.50.
Rashidi said the state government and FAMA would also help distribute surplus vegetables to markets outside Penang, including Kuala Lumpur and Putrajaya, to prevent oversupply.
"Any excess supply will be channelled outside Penang through our network with FAMA," he added.
He said the campaign would be expanded through Rahmah Sales programmes across all state constituencies twice a month, with vegetables included alongside other essential goods.
Rashidi also said efforts were underway to streamline the supply chain by reducing the number of intermediaries involved in marketing local produce.
"If vegetables are purchased directly from farms at RM2 and sold outside at RM3.50 or RM4, farmers can still enjoy fairer returns with fewer middlemen involved," he said.
He said the Ara Kuda project, spanning 600 acres, currently produces about three metric tonnes of vegetables daily involving 94 farmers and around 10 vegetable varieties.
Meanwhile, Chow welcomed the initiative, saying it would strengthen food security efforts while helping manage the rising cost of living.
"We want to ensure a balanced supply chain that benefits farmers, producers, and consumers alike while ensuring stable supply and prices," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Add' call on MR DIY following better 1Q 2026 results – CGS International
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: CGS International Securities Malaysia Sdn Bhd has reiterated its 'Add' call for MR DIY Group (M) Bhd after the company posted improved results in the first quarter ended March 31, 2026 (1Q 2026).
In a research note today, it said CGS International remains positive on MR DIY's growth trajectory. It is maintaining its target price of RM2.09 per share.
"MR DIY currently trades at a 2027 forecast price-to-earnings ratio of 19.4 times. We think it is undemanding versus global peer Dollarama's 33.4 times, with a four to five per cent dividend yield for FY2026-2028 forecast as support.
"Same-store sales growth recovery driving stronger earnings and a significantly higher dividend payout to enhance return on equity are key re-rating catalysts, in our view," it said.
In addition, it said downside risks are fewer store rollouts, causing lower revenue growth and weaker margins.
In a Bursa Malaysia filing yesterday, MR DIY's net profit rose to RM192.02 million in 1Q 2026 from RM174.14 million a year ago, driven by revenue growth and improved gross margins.
The home improvement retailer said its revenue also increased to RM1.37 billion in 1Q 2026 from RM1.25 billion in the same period last year.
"Growth was driven by a 7.7 per cent expansion in our store network, which increased from 1,471 stores on March 31, 2025 to 1,584 stores a year later, alongside positive like-for-like sales supported by strong seasonal demand during the Chinese New Year and Hari Raya festive periods," it said.
It remains confident in the resilience of its business model and its ability to manage potential impacts against global economic uncertainties.
"The group will continue to monitor developments closely and remain agile in its response," it said.
The group also declared an interim dividend of RM151.6 million for 1Q 2026, representing a 14.6 per cent increase year-on-year.
At lunch break today, MR DIY shares eased six sen to RM1.64 with 17.55 million shares traded.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: FMM calls for gazettement of Government Procurement Act without delay
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Federation of Malaysian Manufacturing (FMM) has called for the immediate enforcement of the Government Procurement Act (GPA) 2025, citing global supply chain disruptions and rising cost pressures linked to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.
FMM president Jacob Lee said the Act, which was passed by the Dewan Rakyat on Aug 28, 2025 and subsequently by the Dewan Negara in September 2025, should be gazetted without further delay.
He said the GPA 2025 represents Malaysia's first dedicated legislation governing public procurement, with implementation currently pending the appointment of a commencement date by the Finance Minister through a Federal Gazette notification.
Under Section 1(2), the Act's enforcement is contingent on the gazetting of its commencement date. However, no date has been announced to date.
Lee said FMM had long advocated for a single procurement law to consolidate various treasury instructions, circulars and financial regulations that previously governed public procurement, which lacked consistent statutory enforcement.
He said the GPA 2025 establishes a legally binding framework for transparency, fair competition and accountability across all procuring entities at both the federal and state levels.
"Government procurement, as one of the largest areas of public expenditure, plays a critical role in infrastructure development, public services and national competitiveness.
"A statutory framework mandating open and competitive tendering as the primary procurement method, with documented justification for every award decision, raises the baseline for governance across ministries, agencies and government-linked companies (GLCs)," he said.
He added that a transparent procurement system would strengthen investor confidence, reduce risks of rent-seeking, and enhance Malaysia's position as a credible investment destination.
Lee also called for clarity on subsidiary regulations, including approval thresholds, the transition plan for agencies currently operating under existing treasury instructions, and the readiness of the Government Procurement Appeal Tribunal (GPAT).
He said the current global environment has made reforms more urgent, pointing to disruptions in shipping routes, higher freight and insurance costs, and increased trade diversion risks.
"These pressures have direct cost implications for Malaysian manufacturers dependent on imported inputs."
Lee added that robust verification mechanisms under the GPA framework were essential to protect the integrity of the Buy Malaysian policy.
He also proposed that Clause 29 of the Act include a mandatory Buy Made-in-Malaysia requirement across all ministries, agencies and state governments, with annual public reporting on contract allocations to local manufacturers.
"Tender specifications must remain neutral and must not be designed to exclude qualified local suppliers," he said, calling for a mandatory specification review process overseen by the Procurement Board.
To address trade diversion risks, Lee proposed closer coordination between the Registrar of Government Procurement, the Customs Department and the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry, including a centralised Malaysian manufacturer database linked to e-Perolehan.
"For SMEs, we recommend a single-window supplier registration system, the breakdown of large tenders into smaller lots where feasible, and stricter payment timelines of 30 to 45 days with automatic interest penalties for delays."
He also called for government-linked companies (GLCs) receiving public funds to be subjected to the same standards as government agencies, including annual disclosure of supplier nationality and contract values.
He said consistent application across GLCs was essential to ensure a level playing field under the Act.
"FMM remains ready to engage with the government and the Finance Ministry on implementation and urges all agencies to expedite the Act's enforcement."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China's Xi may visit North Korea as early as next week, Yonhap reports
Article:
SEOUL: Chinese President Xi Jinping may visit North Korea as early as next week and will try to act as a mediator between North Korean leader Kim Jong Un and U.S. President Donald Trump, South Korea's Yonhap news agency reported, quoting a senior government official.
Another government source was cited by Yonhap as saying a team of Chinese security service and protocol officials was in Pyongyang recently and that a visit by Xi late in May or early June was likely. Xi, who hosted Trump last week, will try to act as a mediator between Trump and North Korea's Kim, the senior official was quoted as saying in the report released late on Wednesday.
Trump, who met Kim three times in his first term to negotiate the North's nuclear programme, has said he would be open to meeting the North Korean leader again and that he had a good relationship with him.
South Korea's foreign ministry said the government hopes exchanges between North Korea and China contribute to peace and stability on the Korean peninsula and that China can play a constructive role, while declining to comment directly on the report of Xi's visit.
China is a key economic and political ally of North Korea and the two have made efforts to reinforce ties that had cooled during the Covid-19 pandemic.
North Korean leader Kim visited Beijing last year and stood alongside Xi and Russian President Vladimir Putin at a major military parade. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Restaurant chain director charged with RM1.4 million CBT
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A director of a steamboat restaurant chain pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court here today to criminal breach of trust involving more than RM1.4 million in company funds two years ago.
Yong Pitt Lok, 48, was charged in his capacity as a person entrusted with control over the company's funds with committing the offence involving RM1,468,958.29, which was allegedly transferred into his personal bank account for his own benefit.
The offence was allegedly committed at a premises on Jalan Radin Bagus 8, Bandar Baru Sri Petaling, between March 11 and Oct 24, 2024.
The charge was framed under Section 409 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum prison sentence of 20 years, whipping, and a fine upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Muhammad Zul Hilmi Latiff proposed bail at RM120,000 in one surety.
However, defence counsel Lee Keng Fatt appealed for a lower bail of RM50,000, saying his client is the family's sole breadwinner and has three school-going children.
He said his client poses no flight risk, cooperated fully with the police throughout the investigation, and never failed to appear when called three times to give his statement.
The lawyer also requested that the court not impound his client's passport as he needs to travel abroad for business.
Judge Azrul Darus allowed bail at RM50,000 in one surety and fixed June 26 for mention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Debt collector to be charged over alleged sexual assault of teenager
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A debt collector arrested for allegedly confining, tying up and sexually assaulting a 14-year-old boy at an apartment in Bukit Kemuning, Section 32, will be charged in court soon.
Selangor police chief Datuk Shazeli Kahar said police had received instructions from the Deputy Public Prosecutor's office to proceed with charges against the suspect.
"I have been informed that charging instructions have been issued by the Deputy Public Prosecutor's office and the suspect will be charged as soon as possible.
"Once the charges are filed, we will inform the media so they can obtain further details at the relevant court," Shazeli said at a press conference at the Selangor police headquarters on Thursday.
The suspect, who has a criminal record and was released from prison in April last year, was arrested on Saturday at a rented room in Kuala Lumpur.
He was initially remanded for 3 days until Tuesday, and the remand has since been extended for another 5 days.
It was previously reported that the teenager was allegedly
lured into the apartment
on May 12 after the suspect struck up a conversation and offered him a mobile internet package, on condition that he helped carry items into the unit.
Shah Alam police chief Ramsay Embol previously said checks showed the suspect had also been convicted of rape, wrongful confinement and robbery involving a woman 18 years ago.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 4 arrested over cable theft in viral Semenyih video
Article:
KAJANG: Police have arrested 4 suspects, including a woman, after a viral video showed a cable theft incident in Semenyih yesterday.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Naazron Abdul Yusof said the 41-second clip showed four people allegedly stealing cables using two cars along Jalan Zahra 1, Semenyih, about 5.45am on May 20.
He said police received a report from a local resident living near the scene.
"Following the report, a team from the Kajang police headquarters Criminal Investigation Division arrested 3 men and one woman, aged between 24 and 39, in the Batu 9 and Batu 14 Hulu Langat areas.
"Police also seized 2 vehicles believed to have been used in the incident — a white Honda Jazz and a blue Perodua Myvi — along with several items linked to the theft," he said in a statement today.
Naazron said checks showed the first suspect had a record involving 24 criminal offences and 10 drug-related offences, while the second had 10 criminal offences and four drug-related offences.
He added that two other suspects also had committed criminal offences.
"All suspects have been remanded for 3 days to assist investigations," he said.
The case is being investigated under Section 379 of the Penal Code for theft, which carries a jail term of up to 7 years, a fine, or both upon conviction.
Naazron urged those with information on criminal activities to contact the emergency hotline at 999 or the Kajang district control centre at 03-8911 4243.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Former Sungai Rengit police chief, Larkin station constable, charged with accepting bribes
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Two policemen were charged in the Sessions Court here today with separate counts of soliciting and accepting bribes last year.
Inspector Ihsan Zaidani Hassan, 43, and Constable Muhammad Jefri Din, 25, pleaded not guilty before judge Mohd Azhar Othman.
According to the three charges against Ihsan Zaidani, the then Sungai Rengit police station chief allegedly agreed to accept RM8,000 without consideration from a 70-year-old man through a 49-year-old middleman into his personal bank account.
He allegedly committed the offence knowing it was connected to his official capacity, namely, to refrain from taking legal action against a Thai traditional massage business.
The offences were allegedly committed at Maybank Bhd branches in Taman Molek and Taman Pelangi here between Dec 5, 2024, and April 17, 2025.
He was charged under Section 165 of the Penal Code for a public servant obtaining a valuable item without consideration, which carries a maximum prison sentence of two years, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) prosecuting officer Alice Voon Mei Ling proposed bail at RM10,000 with one surety.
However, defence counsel Mohammad Muzammil Mohammad Hairiri requested a lower bail amount, adding that his client, who is currently attached to the Kota Tinggi district police headquarters, is the sole breadwinner for his wife and school-going children.
For the first charge against Muhammad Jefri, he was accused of corruptly soliciting an RM11,000 bribe from a 54-year-old man.
The bribe was allegedly an inducement for him, as an officer attached to the Larkin police station under the Johor Baru South district police headquarters, not to take action against the man who was suspected of unlawful possession under Section 12(2) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
For the second and third charges, he was accused of corruptly obtaining RM2,505 from two men for the same purpose.
The three offences were allegedly committed at an apartment parking lot and a back alley along Jalan Ulu Air Molek here on June 26, 2025.
The charges were framed under Section 16(a)(B) and Section 17(a) of the MACC Act 2009, punishable under Section 24(1) of the same Act, which provides for a maximum prison term of 20 years and a fine of not less than five times the bribe value or RM10,000, whichever is higher, upon conviction.
MACC prosecuting officer Ameera D'Aneez Mohd Shukari did not offer bail as the offences are non-bailable, but suggested bail be set at RM10,000 with two additional conditions should the court exercise its discretion.
Defence counsel Mohamed Imran Mohamed Sam pleaded for bail, stating his client is the youngest of eight siblings, unmarried, and supports his family on a monthly salary of RM1,200 at the Mersing police station.
"He has health issues but supports his ailing grandmother, unwell mother, and a sister who recently lost her job,"he said.
The court granted bail at RM7,000 each with three additional conditions: the accused must not tamper with witnesses, must report to the nearest police station once a month, and must surrender their international passports.
The court fixed July 10 and July 3 for mention and document submission for the respective cases.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bumi Armada 1Q net profit falls to RM40.11mil on weaker FPSO contributions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bumi Armada Bhd posted a lower net profit of RM40.11 million in the first quarter (1Q) ended March 31, 2026, compared with RM182.76 million a year earlier.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia today, the company said revenue for the quarter fell to RM306.56 million from RM473.96 million a year earlier.
The weaker performance was mainly due to lower contributions from Armada Kraken floating production, storage and offloading (FPSO) and Armada Olombendo FPSO, partly offset by lower finance costs.
"The operations segment's revenue declined mainly due to lower revenue from Armada Kraken FPSO following the commencement of the annual option period charter in April 2025 at a lower charter rate, as well as lower finance lease revenue from Armada Olombendo FPSO, it said.
"Finance lease revenue for year-to-date 2026 decreased due to the accounting treatment of finance leases, where revenue declines over time despite stable charter payments," it said.
Bumi Armada chief operating officer Alexander Brigden said the group continued to operate safely across its fleet, recording zero lost time incidents in the first quarter of 2026.
"Our improving financial position enables us to actively bid for new projects, and buoyant market conditions are providing quality opportunities," he said.
On prospects, Bumi Armada said it plans to aggressively pursue charter extensions for selected vessels while bidding for new projects.
The company also aims to improve the operational performance of its vessels and continue its energy transition pathway towards achieving net-zero emissions by 2050 or earlier, focusing on carbon emission reduction initiatives as well as carbon injection technologies and solutions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police reveal former lecturer scammed of RM749,000 after suspects claimed money laundering link
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU, May 21 — A former lecturer lost RM749,000 after she was deceived by a phone scam last year, Kuala Terengganu district police chief ACP Azli Mohd Noor said.
He added that the victim was contacted on July 13, 2025 by suspects impersonating insurance agents and the police, who accused her of being involved in money laundering and had to pay a sum of money for a Bank Negara investigation.
“She was asked to open two new bank accounts and hand the information to the suspect. She was also instructed to transfer money to several different accounts they provided.
“The victim was afraid of legal action and made payments totalling RM749,000 between August 2025 and January this year using her personal savings and bank loans,” he said in a statement today.
The victim grew suspicious and checked the phone number on the SemakMule app and found that it was owned by a scam syndicate.
She realised that she was scammed and lodged a police report yesterday, he said, adding that the case was being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code.
In another case, Azli said a senior citizen lost RM50,692 to an investment scam on Facebook last April when a suspect offered promised high returns on investments in just three to six hours.
The victim then made a first investment and was informed that he had gained a profit of RM20,039.68. But to withdraw the money, he had to pay RM1,450 as a processing fee.
“He was asked to make more money transfers based on various excuses even after paying the initial fee, till he suffered losses of RM50,392,” he said, adding that the victim lodged a police report yesterday after he realised he was scammed. — Bernama

Title: US denies triggering Norway’s export halt, says it helped integrate missile components for Malaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 21 — The United States has denied any involvement in Norway’s decision to revoke export approval for a naval strike missile system ordered by Malaysia.
The clarification came after reports by Free Malaysia Today and other outlets highlighted Norway’s move to cancel the export permit for the Naval Strike Missile (NSM) system supplied by Kongsberg Defence & Aerospace under a 2018 contract.
Norway had earlier announced that it would limit defence exports to its “allies and closest partners,” prompting speculation that US‑made components embedded in the NSM, including advanced guidance gyroscopes, may have triggered American export controls.
US embassy chargé d’affaires David H Gamble Jr said Washington supported Malaysia’s efforts to strengthen its maritime defence, adding that the US backed Malaysia’s acquisition of a naval strike capability to respond to regional threats and to protect its sovereignty.
“The US remains committed to our robust defence and security relationship with Malaysia and further advancing it through our memorandum of understanding on defence cooperation, 14 regular bilateral and multilateral exercises, professional development, and maritime security assistance,” said Gamble.
He said the US had even facilitated the integration of restricted guidance components for the NSM in 2024 to ensure the system could be delivered to Malaysia.
The US Naval Institute reported that the missiles were initially scheduled for delivery to Malaysia in early March but that Malaysian officials were later “notified of unspecified delays.”
